coredata.dc:title: Water-soluble coumarin oligomer based ultra-sensitive iron ion probe and applications
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jingshi
Guo, Yuan
Dong, Biao
Sun, Jiao
Lyu, Jiekai
Sun, Liheng
Hu, Songtao
Xu, Lin
Bai, Xue
Xu, Wen
Mintova, Svetlana
Song, Hongwei
coredata.dc:description: Organic molecular probes are the main method for metal ion detection because of their excellent selectivity, while the approach remains limited due to the hydrophobic properties of highly efficient fluorescent molecules. Herein, the internal hydrophobic-external hydrophilic molecule design strategy was proposed to improve the water solubility and the sensing performance. Based on a coumarin-molecule-based iron ion probe, we further introduced a central hydrophobic molecule tribromophenol to connect the hydrophobic part of coumarin molecules. By investigating the experimental conditions and molecular structure, the trimeric phenolic coumarin (TPC) molecules with the largest hydrophilic/hydrophobic ratio exhibited excellent water solubility, fluorescent property and ultra-sensitive iron ion detection performance. Greatly enhanced iron ions binding ability lead to ultralow detection limit of 9.6 pM which is much lower than that of coumarin molecule probes and other reported iron ion probes (nM level). And more, this molecular design also enabled sun-excited naked eye Fe3+ detection. Due to the remarkable biocompatibility of the probe, the sensitive intracellular iron ions are achieved with a very low detection limit of 0.2 mM. This molecule design provides a promising and powerful method for enhanced water soluble and effective metal ion detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oligomer probe
Trimeric phenolic coumarin
Iron ion detection
Naked-Eye detection
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the iron ion probe (DAT)
Synthesis of oligomer coumarin-based molecular probes
Comparison of the HOMO-LUMO energy level difference by CV curves
Fe3+ ion sensing detection with spectrometer
Naked-eye Fe3+ ion detection under sunlight excitation
Intracellular Fe2+/Fe3+ ion ratio fluorescence detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis procedure for the initial coumarin-based iron ion probe molecule (DAT) via Perkin reaction.

(a) Structures of DAT, TPC and the corresponding hydrophobic and hydrophilic parts of the molecules. The red line highlights the corresponding hydrophobic part of the molecules. (b) Infrared spectroscopy of DAT, TPC and TBP. (For interpretation of the references to colour in the Figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(a) Fluorescence intensity curves of Fe3+ detection; inset: fluorescence quenching under UV light. (b) The Fe3+ detection curve based on the TPC fluorescence intensity. (c) The Fe3+ selectivity results for TPC. (d) Fluorescence photos for Fe3+ selectivity with other ions under UV light.

(a) DAT emission spectrum. (b) Fluorescence quenching after DAT is combined with iron ions. (c) Recovered DAT emission after EDTA addition. (d) The TPC emission spectrum. (e) Fluorescence quenching after TPC is combined with iron ions. (f) TPC combined with iron ions and the quenched fluorescence. Addition of EDTA made no change.

(a) Theoretical simulations of the HOMO-LUMO energies of DAT and TPC molecules with and without Fe3+. (b) Excitation and emission spectra of DAT and TPC. (c) The theoretical simulation of TPC combines with Fe3+. (d) The TEM images of TPC molecules combined with different concentrations of Fe3+ and the corresponding particle size distribution. (e) The EDX mapping of TPC combined with Fe3+.

Fe3+ outdoor detection with TPC probes in (a) sunny and snowy (b) weather. The TPC solution mixed with different Fe3+ concentration ranges from10 μM–90 μM. Natural light excitation and naked eye detection. (c) Fe3+ (10 μM to 90 μM) detection with a solar simulator and fiber optic spectrometer. (d)Fe3+ (10 μM to 90 μM) detection via the ratio fluorescence of TPC and CsPbBr3 perovskite quantum dots tested with a solar simulator and fiber optic spectrometer.

(a)Spectra recorded with solar simulator and fiber spectrometer within the iron ion concentration range from 10 μM–130 μM. Inset: Indoors and outdoors TPC solution photos, indoor lighting does not have UV light. (b) linear detection results excited by sun light from solar simulator and recorded with a simple fiber spectrometer. The fluorescence intensity corresponds to the Fe3+ concentration within the range from 10 μM–130 μM. Inset: the linear Fe3+ detection within the range from 10 μM–50 μM. (c) Emission spectrum change for the ratio fluorescence measurements with the range from 10 μM–90 μM line. (d) Ratio fluorescence of the Fe3+ detection with the range from 10 μM–90 μM Inset: the linear Fe3+ detection within the range from 10 μM–50 μM.

(a) Iron ion naked-eye detection within the concentration of 0-70 μM tested in a 96-well plate and naked-eye visual Fe3+ resolution tests at 54 μM with a fish shape pattern. (b) Fluorescent anticounterfeiting application experiments using TPC probes under UV light. (c) The MCF-7 cells incubated with TPC probes in confocal culture dishes and the changes after the addition of Fe3+ under UV light. (d) The MTT results of TPC probes with MCF-7 cells (e) Fluorescence imaging with TPC and AO dye with confocal microscope in MCF-7 cells and ratio sensing for Fe3+. Blue channel is for TPC and green channel is for AO dye. (f) Ratio grey intensity value from different channels of AO and TPC for the Fe3+ ion detection. (g) Fluorescence imaging of TPC and AO dye in MCF-7 cells for Fe2+ sensing. (h) Ratio grey intensity value from different channels of AO and TPC for the Fe2+ detection. Ratio fluorescence for the ion detection line. (For interpretation of the references to colour in the Figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
TPC designed through the internal hydrophobic-external hydrophilic strategy achieves greatly enhanced Fe3+ sensing performance, and can be used for naked eye detection with sunlight excitation.

Molecular design of the different substituted coumarin oligomer molecules: TPC, TePC, HPC and MPC and the corresponding MS measurement, luminescent imaging under a UV lamp and the dipole moment determination from CV curves.
Comparison of Fe3+ probes, detection methods and detection performance.

head.ce:title: Water-soluble coumarin oligomer based ultra-sensitive iron ion probe and applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics, College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun, 130012, PR China

National Demonstration Center for Experimental Chemistry Education
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of the Ministry of Education, National Demonstration Center for Experimental Chemistry Education, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Northwest University, Xi'an 710127, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Cell Biology, College of Basic Medical Sciences, Jilin University, Changchun, Jilin, 130021, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Cell Biology, College of Basic Medical Sciences, Jilin University, Changchun, Jilin, 130021, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratoire Catalyse & Spectrochimie, University of Caen, CNRS, 6, boulevard du Maréchal Juin, 14050 Caen Cedex, France
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratoire Catalyse & Spectrochimie, University of Caen, CNRS, 6, boulevard du Maréchal Juin, 14050 Caen Cedex, France

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Trimeric phenolic coumarin (TPC) was designed through the internal hydrophobic-external hydrophilic strategy, achieving greatly enhanced water solubility and sensing performance with detection limit of 9.6 pM, which is much lower than that of coumarin molecule probes and other reported iron ion probes. Sun-excited naked eye Fe3+ detection and Fe3+/Fe2+ detection in the cellular environment were obtained.

Organic molecular probes are the main method for metal ion detection because of their excellent selectivity, while the approach remains limited due to the hydrophobic properties of highly efficient fluorescent molecules. Herein, the internal hydrophobic-external hydrophilic molecule design strategy was proposed to improve the water solubility and the sensing performance. Based on a coumarin-molecule-based iron ion probe, we further introduced a central hydrophobic molecule tribromophenol to connect the hydrophobic part of coumarin molecules. By investigating the experimental conditions and molecular structure, the trimeric phenolic coumarin (TPC) molecules with the largest hydrophilic/hydrophobic ratio exhibited excellent water solubility, fluorescent property and ultra-sensitive iron ion detection performance. Greatly enhanced iron ions binding ability lead to ultralow detection limit of 9.6 pM which is much lower than that of coumarin molecule probes and other reported iron ion probes (nM level). And more, this molecular design also enabled sun-excited naked eye Fe3+ detection. Due to the remarkable biocompatibility of the probe, the sensitive intracellular iron ions are achieved with a very low detection limit of 0.2 mM. This molecule design provides a promising and powerful method for enhanced water soluble and effective metal ion detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Transition metal ions are essential in biological processes and environmental balance [1]. Among them, Fe3+ is an important metal ion that plays a vital role in oxygen uptake as an important component of human hemoglobin, as well as oxygen metabolism and electron transfer [2]. The detection techniques for iron ions mainly include atomic absorption spectrometry [3], ion chromatography [4], liquid chromatography [5], and inductively coupled plasma [6]. However, these methods generally have some common disadvantages such as high cost, time-consuming detection, and complicated operation and instruments, which limit the application in on-site medical and environmental field detection. Therefore, the simple, accurate and in situ detection of iron ions is of great significance for both medical and environmental sciences.

Recently, fluorescence probe-based analytical techniques have become an important tool for metal ion detection [7,8], showing great application prospects in biological [9] and environmental sciences [10], chemical analysis [11], and food science [12]. The detection principle is to convert molecular recognition information after the probe is selectively bonded to the target analyte. Detection is achieved by changes in the fluorescence intensity [13], spectral position [14], or fluorescence lifetime signal [15]. These metal ion fluorescence probes possess important value, especially give their low requirements of operation and equipment and the prospect of on-site detection, such as for community and home-based self-screening.

At present, typical probes mainly involve fluorescent species based on organic small molecules [16,17], semiconductor quantum dots [18], carbon quantum dots [19], and fluorescent metal organic frameworks (MOF) [20]. Among them, organic small molecule fluorophores have attracted great attention for using as metal ion probes because of their good selectivity and sensitivity. Typical organic molecular ion probes include fluorescein [21], rhodamine [22,23], cyanine dyes, boron-dipyrromethene (BODIPY) [24], naphthylamide [25] and coumarins [26,27]. The advantage of these organic small molecule probes is their flexible molecular design, which allows for the precise recognition of ions based on ion size and charge properties [28–30]. Relative to other kinds of ion probes, this atomic-level selectivity provides organic small molecule probes unparalleled properties. In particular, coumarin-based fluorescent probes have gradually become an emerging research hotspot in recent years [31,32]. The coumarin fluorophore contains a benzopyran one structure with a high fluorescence quantum yield, adjustable photophysical and photochemical properties, and high stability [33]. The compound is a great candidate fluorophore for the molecular design of a fluorescent ion probe.

However, small-molecule-based fluorescent probes currently face a common challenge that the luminescent group always includes a benzene ring or heterocyclic ring structure, which is usually hydrophobic, resulting in a decrease in the water solubility of the probe molecule, and thereby, reducing the detection sensitivity [34,35]. Therefore, improving the water solubility of fluorescent probe molecules is an important prerequisite for expanding the ion detection performance and a key problem to be solved [36,37].

In this work, to improve the water solubility of the probe molecule, an internal hydrophobic-external hydrophilic structure has been designed. If several small molecule probes, such as coumarins, are linked through a hydrophobic molecule with the hydrophobic portion facing inward and the hydrophilic portion facing outward, then it is desirable to obtain oligomeric molecules, which can effectively increase the water solubility, enabling a high sensitivity. When this feature was applied, tribromophenol was employed as the internal hydrophobic linking molecule, the trimeric phenolic coumarin (TPC) molecules with the largest hydrophilic/hydrophobic ratio was obtained, exhibiting excellent water solubility and fluorescent property. On the basis of the enhanced water solubility, an ultralow detection limit of 9.6 pM with a high detection sensitivity was achieved, which is three orders of magnitude lower than the current detection level. Due to the superior binding ability of the probe molecule for iron ions, the probe can be excited by sunlight and achieve naked-eye monitoring as shown in Scheme 1 . In addition, due to the excellent water solubility and biocompatibility of the probe, the enhanced sensing of intracellular iron ions was achieved with a very low detection limit of 0.2 mM. The remarkable performance of our TPC molecule Fe3+ probes could lead to further development in water soluble molecule design as well as in home-based self-screening.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the iron ion probe (DAT)
ce:section.ce:para: The initial coumarin-based iron ion probe molecule is N-[2-hydroxy-1,1-bis(hydroxymethyl)ethyl]-7-hydroxycoumarin-3-carboxamide, which is abbreviated as DAT. The structure is composed of the coumarin parent ring and an iron capture group. The synthesis of the DAT molecule is as the previous reported work, including two steps (Fig. 1 ) [38]. In the first step, 6.6 mmol 2,4-Dihydroxybenzaldehyde (purchased from Aladdin) and 4 mmol diethyl malonate were added to 0.1 mL of piperidine and 10 mL of ethanol at 80 °C for 24 h. The coumarin parent ring with ethyl ester was formed via the Perkin reaction [38]. In the second step, 234 mg (1 mmol) above mid-product reacted with 121 mg 2-amino-2-(hydroxymethyl)-1,3-propanediol (1 mmol, purchased from Aladdin) in anhydrous ethanol (5 mL) at 80 °C and refluxed for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was concentrated under vacuum, and the product was purified by column chromatography (CH2Cl2: CH3OH = 10: 1); solid state DAT with a yellow color (185 mg, yield: 60 %) was obtained [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of oligomer coumarin-based molecular probes
ce:section.ce:para: The final iron ion probe molecule has the internal hydrophobic-external hydrophilic structure; the inner part is the benzene ring structure, and the external part contains coumarin-based molecules. This oligomer molecule was obtained by Br atom substitution on the benzene ring. Before the nucleophilic substitution of the Br atom, the iron ion capture groups on DAT molecules were protected by formic acid at 60 °C for 1 h, which is a conventional hydroxy protection method. Several DAT-substituted tribromophenol was obtained by the nucleophilic substitution reaction principle under different reaction conditions.

The substitution reaction was carried out between the hydroxyl group on the parent ring of the coumarin and the Br atom on the benzene ring. Briefly, 0.5 mmol formic-acid-protected DAT was added to 0.1 mmol of tribromophenol and 0.01 mmol catalyst copper iodide (CuI) in 20 mL dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). The mixture was heated at 70 °C for 24 h, and deprotection was carried out by adding 0.1 g of potassium carbonate. The final solution was separated by the column chromatography method, and the composition and ratio of the flowing solvent are CH3COOC2H5 and CH3OH (6:1). The final product is trimeric phenolic coumarin molecules, which can be abbreviated as TPC, with a yield of 80 %.

With this procedure, different substituted forms of the molecule can be obtained at different temperatures. Hex substituted phenoxide coumarin (HPC) was synthesized at 110 °C in DMSO solvent [39,40]. When reducing the reaction temperature to 80 °C and prolonging the reaction time for 24 h, tetrasubstituted phenoxide coumarin (TePC) was obtained. Further reducing the temperature to 70 °C for 24 h yielded TPC. When replacing the parent inner hydrophobic tribromophenol with tribromo benzene, only monosubstituted phenyl coumarin (MPC) was obtained at 110 °C for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the HOMO-LUMO energy level difference by CV curves
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the polarity of the substituted coumarin probe molecules, the relative dipole moment was tested by the highest occupied molecular orbital and lowest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO-LUMO) energy level difference, which reflected the polarity of the molecule. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves were obtained from the electrochemical method. The distance between the first oxidation peak and the first reduction peak is the HOMO-LUMO energy of the molecule.

In the electrochemical measurements, 1 μM of the four molecules was uniformly spin-coated on 2 × 2 cm2 of fluorinated tin oxide (FTO) conductive glass and fixed on the working electrode. A three-electrode system was employed: the working electrode was a glassy carbon electrode, the reference electrode was an Ag / AgCl electrode, and the auxiliary electrode was a platinum wire electrode. Anhydrous dichloromethane and ammonium hexafluorophosphate were used as the electrolyte. The oxygen in the solution was removed by adding nitrogen. The test was carried out using a standard potassium ferricyanide solution to ensure a reversible cyclic voltammogram. The measurement of the cyclic voltammogram was set to a maximum of E = 1 V and a minimum of E = −2 V, and the scan rate was set to 0.1 V/s.

The structure of the compounds was characterized by mass spectra (MS) and Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. MS were recorded on an AXIMA-CFR laser desorption ionization time-of-flight spectrometer (COMPACT). FT-IR spectra were recorded on a Nicolet Impact 410 FT-IR spectrometer in the 400–4000 cm-1 region by mixing the samples with KBr. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a JEOL-2100 F high-resolution transmission electron microscope. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) extinction spectra were measured with a Shimadzu UV-3101PC UV–vis scanning spectrophotometer with a range of 200–1100 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3+ ion sensing detection with spectrometer
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 0.1 mmol of TPC was dissolved in 100 mL of distilled water to form a 1 mM TPC solution. Then, 50 mM Fe3+ standard Fe(NO3)3 solution was prepared, and 5 mM-50 pM Fe3+ standard solutions was then prepared by dilution. In the detection process, 2 mL of Fe3+ solution (50 mM-50 pM) was loaded into a quartz cuvette while adding 1 mL of TPC solution and 1 mL of PBS (pH = 7.4). The fluorescence spectra of TPC mixed with different concentrations of Fe3+ were recorded with a spectrometer. In this work, all the TPC solutions were set to 1 mM, the excitation wavelength was fixed at 365 nm, and a 5 nm slit was installed for both the excitation and emission measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Naked-eye Fe3+ ion detection under sunlight excitation
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, 1 mM Fe3+ solution was prepared. Then, to prepare the test solutions, 630 μL TPC solution was added to 70 μL Fe3+ solution with different concentrations from 100 μM to 900 μM. Each naked-eye test was performed three times. The test place was on the campus of Jilin University, Changchun, China. The three tests were performed under different weather and temperature conditions, and the situation details of the measurement are provided in the following Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular Fe2+/Fe3+ ion ratio fluorescence detection
ce:section.ce:para: The MCF-7 cells were cultured in an incubator at 37 °C with 5 % CO2. The cells were supplemented with 1 % penicillin-streptomycin (100 U/mL penicillin and 100 g/mL streptomycin) and 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS, Clark).

The MCF-7 cells were incubated with 100 μL of TPC in a confocal dish for 3 h to ensure complete endocytosis; the excess TPC was removed. The reference fluorescence was the acridine orange (AO) dye. After incubation with 1 μL of AO dye for 10 min, the cells were washed three times with the cell culture medium to remove excess AO dye and then placed for confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). During the test, 100 μL (0−10 mM) Fe3+ was added dropwise. The AO dye emitted green light under the 488 nm excitation (λex = 488 nm), so the green channel (500 nm–600 nm) was used for the detection of the AO emission. For TPC molecule imaging, a 405 nm laser was employed as the excitation source (λex = 405), and the blue channel served as the detection channel (415 nm–475 nm). The relative intensity of the fluorescence in different channels can be used to monitor the cellular iron ion concentration. For the Fe2+ detection, a different sensing strategy was designed. After the incubation with Fe2+, 10 μL of 5 mM H2O2 was added to the medium, and the cells were incubated with TPC for 3 h. After the oxidation of Fe2+ to Fe3+, the ratio fluorescence test was performed using different channels in the CLSM instrument.

The structural design of organic small molecules is essential for water solubility and the most important feature of water-soluble molecules is their hydrophilic groups. The design strategy for the target molecule is to have hydrophilic groups as more as possible on the outside and the hydrophobic portions inside. Therefore, the ratio of the hydrophilic portions to the hydrophobic portions becomes an important evaluation factor for the water solubility of the oligomeric molecule. This value also has a certain correlation with the polarity. Herein, tribromophenol and tribromo benzene were employed as the internal hydrophobic linking molecules. The reason for the selection of these molecules is based on the following points: the benzene ring can serve as a hydrophobic linking center with a minimized hydrophobic ratio, and by substitution of the Br atoms, the hydrophobic portion of the probe molecule can be linked.

Note that the substitution of Br atoms is an important method to achieve the ether reaction because the size and activity of the Br atom are very suitable and beneficial for molecular grafting, which theoretically allows a benzene ring to link three coumarin molecules. The hydroxyl group on the parent ring of the coumarin can be used to achieve the substitution of the Br atoms, which is key to the water-soluble molecular preparation. In this work, obtaining the highest hydrophilic/hydrophobic ratio by exploring experimental conditions is critical. As shown in Scheme 2 , hydroxyl groups on the benzene ring and different reaction time and temperature were systematically studied in the exploration of water solubility promotion. With the focus on the Br-substituted molecule, under different experimental conditions, different oligomeric molecules with the benzene ring and coumarin complex were obtained, showing different solubility, optical and sensing properties.

First, the hydroxyl group on the central benzene ring has an important influence on the electron cloud structure of the hydrophobic center and on the reactivity of the Br atoms. When tribromo benzene is used as the inner core, only one Br atom of the coumarin probe can be substituted, while the others cannot be replaced. This restriction is due to that the substitution of one Br rapidly increasing the dissociation energies of the other two Br atoms, resulting in the prevention of further substitution reactions, so the solubility remains poor. In the mixed and layered solvents of cyclohexane and water, MPC can exist only in the cyclohexane layer with very weak photoluminescence. When tribromophenol is used as the hydrophobic core, the three Br atoms are more easily substituted by the probes due to the hydroxyl group on the benzene ring, which reduces the dissociation energy of the Br atoms. However, the experimental conditions for nucleophilic substitution need to be precisely controlled. Different structures of multimeric molecules were obtained at different reaction temperatures, as shown in the Table 1 by the corresponding mass spectrometry results. For example, when heated at 110 °C for 2 h, although all substitutions can be realized, since the reaction belongs to p-π conjugation, the degree of polymerization is so high that the electron cloud folds considerably, which makes the electron jumping to an excited state difficult and illumination cannot occur. When the temperature is set at 80 °C, although TePC can emit, the molecule is still not water soluble because of its low ratio of hydrophilic to hydrophobic parts, which means the reaction temperature needs to be further decreased. At 70 °C, which is a low temperature relative to the HPC and TePC conditions, the trisubstituted oligomer molecule-TPC with only one benzene ring is obtained, which is confirmed by the corresponding mass spectrometry. In addition, from the mass spectrum, the molecular weight of TPC is 1078, and the molecular weight of the fragments falling from the three adjacent large peaks is 235, which is just the molecular unit of coumarin. Furthermore, the obtained probe molecules possess an excellent water solubility and remarkable luminescence property compared to other molecules. As the picture shows, the trimer probe molecule totally dissolves in the water layer and emits a bright blue light under UV excitation.

The idea of our molecule design is internally hydrophobic and externally hydrophilic, so the hydrophobic/hydrophilic ratio is a key factor. In the experiments with tribromophenol, although the hydrophilic ion-trapping groups are all facing outward, the internal hydrophobic portion should be as small as possible to increase the overall water solubility. At this point, the TPC oligomer has the smallest hydrophobic proportion, which is critical for the overall water solubility. On the other hand, if the hydrophilic group toward the outside is less, while the internal hydrophobic group is more, the whole molecule exhibits hydrophobicity. This process occurs gradually during a slow temperature rise. As shown in the Scheme 2, when the temperature is raised from 70 °C to 110 °C, the central hydrophobic portion further increases due to a plurality of hydrophobic benzene rings present in the center.

Additionally, the multimeric structure obtained at a high temperature also weakens the overall luminescent intensity. These molecular structures conform to our original design principle. In addition, CV curves were also measured to investigate the HOMO-LUMO energy levels, which further corresponded to the molecular polarity. The corresponding results are listed in Scheme 2. As is known, the distance between the first redox peaks in the CV curve is the HOMO-LUMO energy difference. Experimentally, TPC has a relatively small energy difference, so among the four molecules, TPC presents the highest molecular polarity. Meanwhile, TPC was also confirmed as the most water soluble. Therefore, TPC, with only one benzene ring and the outer three coumarin hydrophilic groups, exhibited the highest hydrophilic/hydrophobic ratio and was further studied for the iron ion detection. The picture of TPC is shown in Fig. S1.

Note that using hydrophilic/hydrophobic ratios to interpret and design molecules is a new strategy for water solubility. However, low-polymerization molecules are beyond the scope of this prediction method since small molecules do not have the repeating structure of macromolecules and polymers, even if they contain a high polarity.

In the structure of DAT, the coumarin parent ring is the hydrophobic part (as marked by the red border), which causes the poor water solubility. When three DAT molecules are connected by the hydrophobic benzene ring structure, then all the hydrophobic parts become aggregated at the center of the oligomer (as marked by the red border of TPC), and the internally hydrophobic and externally hydrophilic structure is formed. When the iron ion capture group is the hydrophilic part, and the other is the hydrophobic part, then TPC has the highest hydrophilic/hydrophobic ratio, which is 4.1, while HPC and TePC yield 4, which also confirms the water solubility prediction strategy.

The structures of DAT and TPC were also confirmed by FT-IR spectra. There is a strong absorption peak at 1604.7 cm−1 (red line), which can be attributed to benzene ring. The strong absorption peak at 1265.30 cm−1 is the absorption peak of phenol (blue line). In the spectra of the three samples, the hydroxyl and CH2 groups were labeled with different colors, indicating successful synthesis of tribromophenol, DAT and TPC. The hydroxyl group on the DAT parent ring disappeared in the TPC, indicating that DAT was linked to the tribromophenol using this hydroxyl group. An absorption peak of diphenyl ether occurs at 1203.58 cm−1, indicating the successful polymerization as shown in Fig. 2 and Fig. S2.

On the basis of good water solubility and luminescent properties, excellent ion sensing performance can be expected. When the TPC molecules are dispersed in water, each molecule has multiple contact sites. In particular, with the gradual addition of Fe3+, the luminescence intensity of the TPC molecules continuously decreases, showing a linear relationship (R2 = 0.992). Thus, when Fe3+ contacts the TPC molecule, the two rapidly recombine and cause the fluorescence quenching38. Among the previous detection probes, the general detection limit is at the nM level with a spectrometer. In this work, the detection limit reaches the pM level, which represents a promotion of three orders of magnitude.

The main reason for the high-efficiency detection comes from the improved water solubility and the further enhanced combination of the probe and iron ions. The solubility of the probe in the solvent is a prerequisite; while the high-strength interaction with iron ions is more important for the improved selectivity and detection limit. A comparison experiment was conducted between TPC and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) which is a very famous metal ion chelating ligand, to explore the chelating ability toward Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 4 , the chelation ability of TPC and DAT molecules for Fe3+ was tested with excess EDTA ligands. In the group of DAT probes, the DAT molecule was fully reacted with Fe3+ firstly, and then, EDTA was added dropwise to the solution. Evidently, the completely quenched fluorescence recovers as upon the addition of EDTA to the solution, which means that EDTA can capture the iron ions from the DAT monomer probe. On the contrary, in the experiments of TPC molecules, EDTA cannot restore the fluorescence of TPC molecules under the same conditions, indicating the very intense and irreversible iron ion binding capacity of TPC, surpassing even that of the EDTA ligand. Therefore, the chelating ability of TPC is substantially greater than that of DAT, indicating that the considerable Fe3+ sensing ability comes from not only the enhanced water solubility but also the enhanced iron ion binding capacity.

In addition, in the process of exploring Fe3+ detection, we also explored the detection conditions in other polar solvents (such as methanol and ethanol), and the detection results are not as high as that with the water. On the one hand, this result is probably due to Fe3+ ions being in nonaqueous systems, where the solubility is not high. On the other hand, the organic solvent is prone to complex coordination reactions with Fe3+, which affects the true Fe3+ detectability.

To investigate the detection and the fluorescence quenching mechanism, theoretical calculations were carried out, as shown in Fig. 5 a. The electron distribution of the HOMO, LUMO and LUMO + 4 of the complex was calculated by B3LYP/6−31 G (d, p). After the combination with Fe3+, the electron distribution of the composite LUMO + 4 is extended to Fe, indicating the electron transfer from HOMO to LUMO + 4. By theoretically calculating the HOMO-LOMO orbital energy levels, the molecular orbital energy of TPC is found to be extremely poor compared with that of the DAT molecule, while the binding energy for TPC and iron ions is less than the energy gap of the DAT molecule. The excitation and emission spectra of DAT molecules and TPC molecules are shown in Fig. 5b. The energy gap of the TPC molecule is less than that of the DAT molecule, which can confirm the red shift of the excitation spectrum. Comparing the absorption (Fig. S3) and the excitation spectra (Fig. S4) during the combination of TPC probes and Fe3+, gradual fluorescence quenching can be found, which is accompanied by the aggregation of TPC. Since the formation of the TPC molecule comes from the coupling of DAT and tribromophenol, the emission property of the probes is not affected. Although the sensing performance of the trimer is improved, the main part of detection and luminescence remains with DAT.

To verify the specific binding mode for the TPC molecule and Fe3+, the theoretical simulation of the TPC binding method was also simulated. We find that a polymerization of multiple organic molecules via the iron ions happens during the sensing, which is similar to biological triaging [54–56]. From a small amount to 24 and 60 TPC molecules, TPC always forms spherical aggregates via iron ions eventually, as shown by the simulation results in Fig. 5c. Therefore, an irreversible combination of TPC and Fe3+ can be obtained, which greatly enhances the sensitivity by 5 orders of magnitude relative to that of the DAT monomer. The morphology of the aggregates was analyzed in the presence of different iron ions. As shown in Fig. 5d, TPC molecules were mixed with 1 μM Fe3+ −1 mM Fe3+ and observed by TEM. The concentration of TPC and Fe3+ conjugates is found to gradually increase with the Fe3+ concentration, which further confirms the simulation results. Furthermore, the mapping and EDX analysis in TEM determine that the distribution of Fe species at the center of the unit is highest, which verifies the combination of TPC molecules and Fe, as shown in Fig. 5e.

To ensure sufficient drinking water resources, metal ion detection has become an important part of environmental water quality analysis. When the Fe3+ ion reaches 10 μM or more in water, the pH of the water body rises, and insoluble matter is easily formed. The current research is important for the detection of Fe3+ ions in the environment. TPC's superior iron ion binding ability and performance can also reduce its excitation power relative to most metal probes, even in the case of sunlight excitation, achieving efficient naked eye detection as shown in Fig. S5. Outdoor natural-light-excited Fe3+ ions naked-eye detection was conducted under different weather conditions, as shown in Fig. 6 a and b. In the test range of 10 μM–90 μM, the lower limit of detection of the naked eye reaches 50 μM, which is of great significance for environmental metal ion detection. To further qualitatively explore the Fe3+ ion detection under outdoor conditions, a portable optical fiber spectrometer was used to explore the detection lines up to 10 μM under a solar-powered simulator which fully simulated solar energy (solar constant:1), as shown in Fig. 6c and d.

Fig. 7 a and b show the result excited by the solar simulator. The test range is 10 μM–90 μM, within that the fluorescence intensity of the probe gradually decreases. The spectra were tested by a simple fiber spectrometer. The lower limit of detection of 10 μM and good linear ship were obtained. Since the natural light intensity changed with time, for more precise testing, we employed a ratio fluorescence detection with CsPbBr3 quantum dots which emitted stable green light with sunlight, and the detection limit remained at 10 μM with satisfactory linear ship (Fig. 7c and d).

More applications can be found for TPC such as anti-counterfeiting and cellular Fe3+ detection, because of the efficient detection performance as shown in Fig. S6. The naked-eye detection of TPC probes under UV light was tested with different concentrations of Fe3+ ions as shown in Fig. 8 a. Within 96-well plates, the Fe3+ concentrations range from 0.06 μM–70 μM were tested and the gradual change of the blue light was seen, corresponding to the Fe3+ concentrations. Since efficient illumination can be achieved, a fish-shaped pattern can be made and for sure another pattern can be easily designed (Fig. 8a). TPC molecules are colorless no matter powder or solution, so they can be evenly casted onto paper or other objects without affecting the surface. We can use iron ion solution as the “ink” to write on the paper, and it is not visible indoors since no UV light, but in the case of ultraviolet light or outdoor, the information by iron ions can be read clearly. On the contrary, TPC solution can also serve as the “ink” to give the information on the paper with Fe3+ solution casting. Both of the cases are described in Fig. 8b, demonstrating that TPC molecules have remarkable anti-counterfeiting capabilities based on their fluorescent properties. It is known that Fe3+ is involved in many life activities, such as oxygen transport and redox regulation. Precise detection of Fe3+ in cells is essential since the concentration of Fe3+ usually reflects the active state of cells. To ensure the good biocompatibility, the intracellular binding of TPC with Fe3+ can be observed. When iron ions are added, the color of the cells changes from blue to yellow due to the TPC/Fe3+ complex, as shown in Fig. 8c. MTT cytotoxicity experiments in the cellular environment were conducted (Fig. 8d). The experiments were performed by using the commonly employed live-cell dye acridine orange (AO) and TPC for ratio fluorescence detection. Because AO is not affected by iron ions, while TPC will gradually decrease with the increase of iron ions. Since the fluorescence of AO and TPC can be presented in different channels (AO: green channel, TPC: blue channel), so the ratio of gray value of fluorescence values from different channels allows for the detection of iron ions in cells.

In the cellular detection of Fe3+, the state of the cells can also be observed. In the Fe3+ tests, the concentration range was 0.2−1 mM, and the detection limit reached 0.2 mM (Fig. 8e and f). The cells maintained a high degree of viability throughout the test, demonstrating that the TPC probe has good biological compatibility. At the same time, with the addition of a lower concentration of H2O2 solution, we indirectly tested the content of Fe2+ in the cells. Consequently, the test range is determined as 0.5 mM–1.5 mM, and the lower limit of detection is 0.5 mM (Fig. 8g and h). The detection of Fe2+/Fe3+ in cells has important significance for research concerning redox reactions in cells.

In this work, external-hydrophobic/internal-hydrophilic strategy was employed for improving the water solubility of hydrophobic molecule metal ion probes. Central hydrophobic molecule, tribromophenol, is used to combine three coumarin molecules achieving the highest hydrophilic/hydrophobic ratio by exploring experimental conditions. Since the hydrophilic group is directed outward, greatly enhancing the molecular water solubility, ultralow detection limit of 9.6 pM was obtained, which is much lower than that of the other reported iron ion probes (nM level). In addition, solar-excited naked eye Fe3+ detection can be realized due to the molecular design and the following remarkable binding ability and the luminescent efficiency. Remarkable biocompatibility of the TPC probe also enable the sensitive ratio intracellular Fe3+ and Fe2+ detection with very low detection limits of 1.5 mM and 1.0 mM, respectively. This molecule design strategy can be extended to design a universal hydrophobic probe, and find a wide-range application in the areas of environmental and health assessment.
Supporting Information

Condition of the outdoor detection of Fe3+ by the naked-eye under natural light, as well as indoor detection. (Table S1). Excitation spectra of TPC molecule probes with different Fe3+ concentrations (Fig. S1). Absorption spectra of TPC molecule probes with different Fe3+ concentrations (Fig. S2). Infrared spectra of TPC (Fig. S3). Absorption spectra of TPC molecule probes with different Fe3+ concentrations. (Fig. S4). Color changes of TPC molecules with different Fe3+ amount under nature light. (Fig. S5). Color changes when Fe3+ were added in the cells which incubated with TPC molecules. (Fig. S6).

Jingshi Liu conducted the most of investigation for the samples and wrote the main paper. Biao Dong, Jiao Sun, Hongwei Song supervised the project, had given valuable advices on the proceeding of this work, and revised the manuscript. Yuan Guo prepared the DAT molecules. Lin Xu Xue Bai and Svetlana Mintova had provided precious suggestions on analysis of experimental data about preparation of materials and iron ion sensor. Jiekai Lyu, Liheng Sun and Songtao Hu conducted IR measurements and some sensing experimetns. Wen Xu supported the characterization of the samples. All authors discussed the results and commented on the manuscript at all stages.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Xie, X. Sun, J. Jiao, X. Xin, Ionic liquid-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of iron ion and ascorbic acid, Colloids and Surfaces a-Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects, 529(2017) 38-44.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Drug-Derived Bright and Color-Tunable N-Doped Carbon Dots for Cell Imaging and Sensitive Detection of Fe3+in Living Cells
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Song, C.Z. Zhu, J.H. Song, H. Li, D. Du, Y.H. Lin, Drug-Derived Bright and Color-Tunable N-Doped Carbon Dots for Cell Imaging and Sensitive Detection of Fe3+ in Living Cells, Acs Applied Materials & Interfaces, 9(2017) 7399-405.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A long-lived phosphorescence iridium(III) complex as a switch on-off-on probe for live zebrafish monitoring of endogenous sulfide generation
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.-N. Ko, C. Yang, S. Lin, S. Li, Z. Dong, J. Liu, et al., A long-lived phosphorescence iridium(III) complex as a switch on-off-on probe for live zebrafish monitoring of endogenous sulfide generation, Biosens Bioelectron, 94(2017) 575-83.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly selective "turn-on" fluorescent sensing of fluoride ion based on a conjugated polymer thin film-Fe3+complex
Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.C. Ding, J.K. Xu, Y.P. Wen, J. Zhang, H.T. Liu, Z.X. Zhang, Highly selective "turn-on" fluorescent sensing of fluoride ion based on a conjugated polymer thin film-Fe3+complex, Anal Chim Acta, 967(2017) 78-84.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile construction of imidazole functionalized polysiloxanes by thiol-ene “Click” reaction for the consecutive detection of Fe3+ and amino acids
Sens Actuator B-Chem

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.J. Zuo, Y. Zhang, Z.M. Gou, W.Y. Lin, Facile construction of imidazole functionalized polysiloxanes by thiol-ene "Click" reaction for the consecutive detection of Fe3+ and amino acids, Sens Actuator B-Chem, 291(2019) 235-42.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structure and assembly of clathrin cages
Subcell. Biochem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Halebian, K. Morris, C. Smith, Structure and Assembly of Clathrin Cages, Sub-cellular biochemistry, 83(2017) 551-67.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A triskelion-shaped saddle-helix hybrid nanographene
Angew Chem-Int Edit

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.M. Cruz, I.R. Marquez, S. Castro-Fernandez, J.M. Cuerva, E. Macoas, A.G. Campana, A Triskelion-Shaped Saddle-Helix Hybrid Nanographene, Angew Chem-Int Edit, 58(2019) 8068-72.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bithiophene triarylborane dyad: an efficient material for the selective detection of CN- and F- ions
Appl. Organomet. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Perumal, S. Karuppannan, S. Gandhi, S. Subramanian, A. Govindasamy, S.K. Gopal, Bithiophene triarylborane dyad: An efficient material for the selective detection of CN- and F- ions, Appl Organomet Chem, 34(2020) 9.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jingshi Liu received his M.S. degree from Jilin University of China, in 2019. His research interest is biosensing properties of organic compound.
Guo Yuan has a doctorate in organic chemistry of Northwest University in 2006, the current Northwest University associate professor of chemistry college.

Biao Dong received his Ph.D degree from Changchun Institute of Physics, CAS in 2008, then he joined the college of Jilin University of electronic science and engineering college work, in 2018 was hired as professor, doctor tutor.
Jiao Sun has a doctorate in physical chemistry of Jilin University in 2007, the current Jilin University assistant professor of basic medicine college.

Jiekai Lyu received his B.S. degree from Jilin University of China, in 2016. He is currently studying for a Ph.D degree at Jilin University. His research interest is the biosensing properties of nanomaterials.

Liheng Sun received his B.S. degree from Jilin University of China, in 2017. He is currently studying for a M.S degree at Jilin University. His research interest is the biosensing properties of nanomaterials.

Songtao Hu received his B.S. degree from Jilin University of China, in 2017. He is currently studying for a M.S degree at Jilin University. His research interest is the biosensing properties of nanomaterials.

Lin Xu has a doctorate in physics electronics of Jilin University in 2011, the current Jilin University associate professor, master tutor, institute of electronics, deputy director of the department of biomedical engineering, jilin university youth science and technology association member.

Xue Bai received her Ph.D degree from Changchun Institute of Physics, CAS in 2008, then into the Jilin University of electronic science and engineering college work, in 2011 was hired as associate professor, master tutor.
Wen Xu received his Ph.D degree from Jilin Univercity, in 2011. She was hired as lecturer, master tutor.

Svetlana Mintova, Dr at University of Caen, is a director of French National Center for scientific research, the chairman of the international molecular sieve Association and a member of the French Council of molecular sieves. Her research areas include porous materials, molecular sieves, colloid chemistry, nano clusters, functional thin films and coatings, nanocomposites and hybrid materials, host guest chemistry, sensing materials and so on.

Hongwei Song received his Ph.D degree from Changchun Institute of Physics, CAS in 1996. From 1996 to 2000, he worked as a postdoctor in Institute of Physics, CAS, Nagoya Institute of Technology and University of California at Berkeley, respectively. In 2001, He gained One hundred Talent Youth Project and became a professor of CIOMP. In 2008, he won National Talent Youth Project of China. He is currently working as a professor in Jilin University. His research interests concern on the synthesis of nanomaterials, their optical and electrical properties and biological applications. So far, he has published more than 160 SCI journal papers and two book chapters.
